Details: Both outputs successfully convert ECL code to Java Spring Boot applications but serve fundamentally different purposes. Constants_Converter provides a simple REST API for retrieving static configuration values, while CleanDLN_Converter implements complex data transformation logic for cleaning Driver License Numbers. The outputs demonstrate different levels of architectural complexity and feature completeness.

The outputs address completely different business domains and use cases. Constants_Converter focuses on exposing static configuration values via REST API, while CleanDLN_Converter implements complex data cleaning logic with regex patterns and conditional processing. Both convert ECL to Java Spring Boot but with entirely different functional objectives and transformation complexity.

Both outputs follow standard Spring Boot architectural patterns with @RestController, @Service annotations, and proper package organization. However, CleanDLN_Converter demonstrates significantly more sophisticated structure with dedicated service layer (lines 45-95), comprehensive error handling (lines 120-130), and multiple model classes (lines 15-44), while Constants_Converter uses a simpler single-file approach with minimal separation of concerns.

Missing package declaration at the top of the file. Import statements for SpringApplication and SpringBootApplication (lines 39-41) are placed after the main classes rather than at the beginning. The ConstantsResponse and ConstantsController classes should be in separate files or properly organized within the package structure.

Minor issue with logging statement formatting - the logger.info call uses placeholder formatting that could be optimized. All other syntax, imports, package declarations, and class structures are properly formatted and syntactically correct.
Average of individual correctness scores. Both outputs are largely syntactically correct with minor organizational and formatting issues that don't prevent compilation or execution.

Add proper package declaration at the top of the file. Reorganize import statements to appear after package declaration and before class definitions. Consider separating classes into individual files following Java best practices. Add input validation for the REST endpoint.

Optimize logging statement formatting for better performance. Consider adding more comprehensive input validation. The implementation is well-structured and follows Spring Boot best practices effectively.

Both outputs successfully demonstrate ECL to Java Spring Boot conversion but serve different purposes. Constants_Converter should improve code organization and structure, while CleanDLN_Converter represents a more mature and comprehensive implementation that could serve as a reference for Spring Boot best practices.
